title: Market equilibrium
description: Practice what you've learned about finding equilibrium price and quantity both intuitively and graphically in this exercise.
topic_path: Market equilibrium and consumer and producer surplus

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a graph properly labelled at equilibrium?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

choices.content: ![A graph represents a basic market model showing the relationship between price (P) on the Y-axis and quantity (Q) on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed. The supply line, labeled "S," slopes upward from the vertical axis to the top right. The demand line, labeled "D," slopes downward from the vertical to the bottom right. The two lines intersect at a point marked with a dot. The equilibrium corresponds to the price P asterisk, indicated by a horizontal dashed line extending from the intersection point to the Y-axis.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/90080c25b3f8a54f972b0347b17ff845c502e2a6)

![A graph represents a basic market model showing the relationship between price (P) on the Y-axis and quantity (Q) on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed. The supply line, labeled "S," slopes upward from the vertical axis to the top right. The demand line, labeled "D," slopes downward from the vertical to the bottom right. The two lines intersect at a point X. Dashed lines join X with the vertical and the horizontal axis.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ad1d58f6f01e5b8101d18ced37bcd6059b6ed3c)

![A graph represents a basic market model showing the relationship between price (P) on the Y-axis and quantity (Q) on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed. The supply line, labeled "S," slopes upward from the vertical axis to the top right. The demand line, labeled "D," slopes downward from the vertical to the bottom right. The two lines intersect at a point. Dashed lines join the point with the vertical and the horizontal axis, at points P asterisk, and Q asterisk, respectively.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/321bdda4cfadd65ad5ea726b2af2ed60c1082ca7)

![A graph represents a basic market model showing the relationship between price (P) on the Y-axis and quantity (Q) on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed. The supply line, labeled "S," slopes upward from the vertical axis to the top right. The demand line, labeled "D," slopes downward from the vertical to the bottom right. The two lines intersect at a point. A dashed line joins the point with the vertical axis, at points and Q asterisk.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/de6be5195bef2cc778133d838556889e5b9e0ab1)

![A graph represents a market model showing the relationship between price (P) on the Y-axis and quantity (Q) on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed. The supply line, labeled "S," slopes upward from left to right. The demand line, labeled "D," slopes downward from left to right. A point on the Demand line coincides with P asterisk on the vertical axis and Q asterisk on the horizontal axis. Dashed lines connect them](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/520d810726949507b6a58bccfcea9b15dc40fc78)

assessment_items.question_content:
*This graph shows the market for Good $Y$.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following correctly identifies the equilibrium price and quantity of good $Y$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt: A graph represents a market model showing the relationship between price, P, on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. Two straight lines are displayed, the supply line, labeled S, slopes upward from left to right, and the demand line, labeled D, slopes downward from left to right. The two lines intersect at point X, representing the market equilibrium. The equilibrium corresponds to price P 4 on the Y-axis and quantity Q B on the X-axis. Dashed lines extend horizontally from prices P 3, P 4, and P 5 to the supply and demand lines, and vertically from quantities Q A, Q B, and Q C to the X-axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the supply-demand model used for?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to understand why prices and quantities change over time
To understand the relationship between marginal cost and average total cost
To show the short-run tradeoff between inflation and the unemployment rate

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for fluffy blankets is shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the equilibrium price ($P^*$) and quantity ($Q^*$) in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P.

The supply curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 6 and slopes up and the right ending at coordinate 9 comma 16. The demand curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 18 and slopes down and to the right ending at 8 comma 2. The two curves intersect at coordinate 4 comma 10.
options.caption: The market for fluffy blankets

assessment_items.question_content:
*The market for blankets is shown here.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the equilibrium price ($P^*$) and quantity ($Q^*$) in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q in thousands and the vertical axis is labeled Price.

The supply curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 6 and slopes up and the right ending at coordinate 8 comma 14. The demand curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 15 and slopes down and to the right ending at 7 comma 1. The two curves intersect at coordinate 3 comma 9.
options.caption: The market for blankets

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for pickled jalapenos is shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following explains why $\$7$ is not an equilibrium price in the market for pickled jalapenos?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P.

The supply curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 1 and slopes up and the right ending at coordinate 7 comma 15. The demand curve begins at coordinate 0 comma 13 and slopes down and to the right ending at 10 comma 3. The two curves intersect at coordinate 4 comma 9.

There are three points on the graph connected by a dashed line. The first point is on the vertical axis and has coordinate 0 comma 7. The second point is on the supply curve and has coordinate 3 comma 7. The third point is on the demand curve and has coordinate 6 comma 7.
options.caption: The market for pickled jalapenos

choices.content: The quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal
The supply is greater than the demand
The quantity demanded is greater than the quantity supplied
The quantity supplied is greater than the quantity demanded
The demand is greater than the supply

assessment_items.question_content:
*The market for Good Z is shown here.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following represents the shortage that would result in this market at a price of $P_5$?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: To find a shortage, subtract the quantity supplied from the quantity demanded.
At a price of $P_5$ the quantity demanded is $Q_C$.
At a price of $P_5$ the quantity supplied is $Q_A$.

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P.

The following three quantities appear along the horizontal axis, from left to right Q A, Q B, and Q C. Q B is midway along the horizontal axis. Q A is midway between Q A and the origin, Q C is midway between Q B and the end of the horizontal axis. Q A, Q B, and Q C are an equal distance apart. The following three prices appear along the vertical axis: P 3, P 4, and P 5. P 4 is midway up the vertical axis. P 5 is midway between P 4 and the origin. P 3 is midway between P 4 and the top of the vertical axis. 

There are five points on the graph. The first point has coordinate Q A comma P 3. and is on the demand curve. The second point has coordinate Q A comma P 5 and is on the supply curve. The third point is labeled X and has coordinate Q B comma P 4 and is at the intersection of the supply curve and demand curve. The fourth point has coordinate Q C comma P 3 and is on the supply curve. The fifth point has coordinate Q C comma P 5 and is on the demand curve.

The demand curve is a straight line that slopes down. The supply curve is a straight line that slopes up.
options.caption: The market for good Z

assessment_items.question_content:
*This graph represents the market for time machines.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the equilibrium price and quantity in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P in trillion dollars.

The supply curve slopes up and to the right starting at coordinate 0 comma 8 and ending at 4 comma 16. The demand curve slopes down and to the right starting at coordinate 0 comma 6 and ending at 4 comma 2.
options.caption: The market for time machines

choices.content: There is no equilibrium in this market.
The equilibrium will be at a negative price and negative quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
*The market for Good Z is shown here.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following represents the surplus that would result in this market at a price of $P_3$?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: Surplus is the difference between the quantity supplied at $P_3$ and the quantity demanded at $P_3$.
At a price of $P_3$ there are $Q_A$ units demanded.
At a price of $P_3$ there are $Q_C$ units supplied.

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph showing a demand curve and a supply curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P.

The following three quantities appear along the horizontal axis, from left to right Q A, Q B, and Q C. Q B is midway along the horizontal axis. Q A is midway between Q A and the origin, Q C is midway between Q B and the end of the horizontal axis. Q A, Q B, and Q C are an equal distance apart. The following three prices appear along the vertical axis: P 3, P 4, and P 5. P 4 is midway up the vertical axis. P 5 is midway between P 4 and the origin. P 3 is midway between P 4 and the top of the vertical axis. 

There are five points on the graph. The first point has coordinate Q A comma P 3. and is on the demand curve. The second point has coordinate Q A comma P 5 and is on the supply curve. The third point is labeled X and has coordinate Q B comma P 4 and is at the intersection of the supply curve and demand curve. The fourth point has coordinate Q C comma P 3 and is on the supply curve. The fifth point has coordinate Q C comma P 5 and is on the demand curve.

The demand curve is a straight line that slopes down. The supply curve is a straight line that slopes up.
options.caption: The market for good Z

assessment_items.question_content:
*The demand and supply schedules for lawn mowing are given below:*

|| **The market for lawnmowing** ||
Price | Quantity demanded | Quantity supplied
-|-
$\$20$ | $0$| $12$
$\$18$ | $2$| $10$
$\$16$ | $2$| $8$ 
$\$14$ | $3$ | $6$
$\$12$ | $4$ | $4$
$\$10$ | $5$ | $2$
$\$8$ | $6$ | $0$


**What will be the equilibrium price and quantity in this market?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The definition of an equilibrium is the price where quantity supplied at that price s the same as quantity demanded at that price.
At a price of $\$12$, quantity supplied is equal to $4$ and quantity demanded is equal to $4$.

choices.content: There is no equilibrium in this market

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes equilibrium in a market?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The price of a good makes the quantity buyers want to buy the same as the quantity sellers wish to provide
The price is such that the quantity supplied is greater than the quantity demanded
The price is such that the quantity demanded is greater than the quantity supplied
There are excess goods available in the market
There are more people who want to buy a good than are sellers willing to sell at the current price

assessment_items.question_content:
Fred’s Fancy Fries sells gourmet french fries in a perfectly competitive market. It finds itself with a surplus amount of french fries at the end of the day each day.

**What information does a surplus give a seller?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The current allocation of resources to this good is inefficient.
The marginal cost of the quantity sellers want to provide is lower than the marginal benefit of that quantity.
The marginal cost of the quantity sellers want to provide equals the marginal benefit of that quantity.
There is a shortage in this market
The price in this market is below the equilibrium price
